title: Computational complexity of optimizing large-market obviously strategy-proof trading mechanisms
background_latex:
Consider a task-allocation problem with a finite set of task types \(i\in\{1,\dots,I\}\) and a finite set of agents \(j\in\{1,\dots,J\}\). Let \(n_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\) denote the total number of instances of task \(i\). An (expected) assignment for agent \(j\) is a vector \(x_j\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\), where \(x_j(i)\) is the expected number of tasks of type \(i\) assigned to \(j\). The designer has a known cost (or negative value) vector \(\pi_j\in\mathbb{R}^I\), so the designer’s total expected social cost is \(\sum_{j\in J}\pi_j\cdot x_j\). Each agent \(j\) has a random private cost vector \(c_j\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\) (drawn independently across \(j\) from a distribution with full support on a hypercube), and the agent prefers lower workload \(c_j\cdot x_j\). The status quo (outside option) is an endowment assignment \(\eta_j\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\) for each agent.

A (single-agent) polarized-ray menu for agent \(j\) consists of a finite set of trading sets \(\{T^k_j\}_{k\in K_j}\) such that either:

1) a remedial menu \(K_j=\{0,1\}\) with \(T^0_j=\{\eta_j\}\) and \(T^1_j=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}: x\le \eta_j\}\), or

2) each \(T^k_j\) is a non-monotone ray with endpoint \(\eta_j\), i.e.
\[T^k_j=\{\eta_j+y\kappa^k_j: y\in\mathbb{R}_{+}\}\]
for some direction \(\kappa^k_j\in\mathbb{R}^I\) with \(\kappa^k_j\not\ge 0\) and \(\kappa^k_j\not\le 0\), and the collection of directions is polarized in the sense that for any \(k',k''\in K_j\) there exist scalars \(y',y''\ge 0\), not both zero, such that \(y'\kappa^{k'}_j+y''\kappa^{k''}_j\ge 0\) (componentwise).

Given such a menu, the agent’s (obviously strategy-proof) behavior is myopic: for each realized \(c_j\), the agent chooses an index \(k\in K_j\) that minimizes \(\min\{c_j\cdot x: x\in T^k_j\}\). The designer then selects an assignment point \(P_j(k)\in T^k_j\). In the large-market relaxation, feasibility requires only market clearing in expectation:
\[\sum_{j\in J}\mathbb{E}[P_j(s_j(c_j))] = (n_1,\dots,n_I).\]
Define \(F_j\subset\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\) as the convex hull of all expected assignments \(\mathbb{E}[P_j(s_j(c_j))]\) achievable for agent \(j\) by varying \(\{T^k_j\}_{k\in K_j}\), the myopically-optimal strategy \(s_j\), and the selection rule \(P_j\), subject to the polarized-ray (or remedial) restriction above. Let \(S_j(w)=\max\{w\cdot y: y\in F_j\}\) be the support function of \(F_j\).

The large-market OSP design problem can be written as the convex program
\[\min_{(x_j)_{j\in J}}\ \sum_{j\in J}\pi_j\cdot x_j\quad\text{s.t.}\quad x_j\in F_j\ \forall j,\ \sum_{j\in J}x_j=(n_1,\dots,n_I),\]
with dual
\[\sup_{\lambda\in\mathbb{R}^I}\ \sum_{i=1}^I\lambda_i n_i-\sum_{j\in J}S_j(\lambda-\pi_j).\]
Evaluating or optimizing over the mechanism for each agent \(j\) therefore reduces to characterizing the support function \(S_j\), i.e. solving an optimization problem whose decision variables are menus of polarized rays and selection rules.
problem_definition_latex: Determine whether the optimization problem of characterizing \(S_j(w)=\max\{w\cdot y: y\in F_j\}\) (equivalently, optimizing over polarized-ray menus and selection rules for a given agent \(j\) in the large-market relaxation) can be solved efficiently as a function of \((w,\eta_j,\text{the distribution of }c_j)\). In particular, establish the computational complexity of computing \(S_j(w)\) (e.g. polynomial-time solvable vs. NP-hard) and/or design an algorithm with provable complexity guarantees for approximating \(S_j(w)\) to a specified accuracy.
latex:
Consider a task-allocation problem with a finite set of task types \(i\in\{1,\dots,I\}\) and a finite set of agents \(j\in\{1,\dots,J\}\). Let \(n_i\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\) denote the total number of instances of task \(i\). The designer has a known cost (or negative value) vector \(\pi_j\in\mathbb{R}^I\), so the designer’s total expected social cost is \(\sum_{j\in J}\pi_j\cdot x_j\). Each agent \(j\) has a random private cost vector \(c_j\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\) (drawn independently across \(j\) from a distribution with full support on a hypercube), and the agent prefers lower workload \(c_j\cdot x_j\). The status quo (outside option) is an endowment assignment \(\eta_j\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\) for each agent.

A (single-agent) polarized-ray menu for agent \(j\) consists of a finite set of trading sets \(\{T^k_j\}_{k\in K_j}\) such that either:

1) a remedial menu \(K_j=\{0,1\}\) with \(T^0_j=\{\eta_j\}\) and \(T^1_j=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^I_{+}: x\le \eta_j\}\), or

\[T^k_j=\{\eta_j+y\kappa^k_j: y\in\mathbb{R}_{+}\}\]
for some direction \(\kappa^k_j\in\mathbb{R}^I\) with \(\kappa^k_j\not\ge 0\) and \(\kappa^k_j\not\le 0\), and the collection of directions is polarized in the sense that for any \(k',k''\in K_j\) there exist scalars \(y',y''\ge 0\), not both zero, such that \(y'\kappa^{k'}_j+y''\kappa^{k''}_j\ge 0\) (componentwise).

Given such a menu, the agent’s (obviously strategy-proof) behavior is myopic: for each realized \(c_j\), the agent chooses an index \(k\in K_j\) that minimizes \(\min\{c_j\cdot x: x\in T^k_j\}\). The designer then selects an assignment point \(P_j(k)\in T^k_j\). In the large-market relaxation, feasibility requires only market clearing in expectation:
\[\sum_{j\in J}\mathbb{E}[P_j(s_j(c_j))] = (n_1,\dots,n_I).\]
Define \(F_j\subset\mathbb{R}^I_{+}\) as the convex hull of all expected assignments \(\mathbb{E}[P_j(s_j(c_j))]\) achievable for agent \(j\) by varying \(\{T^k_j\}_{k\in K_j}\), the myopically-optimal strategy \(s_j\), and the selection rule \(P_j\), subject to the polarized-ray (or remedial) restriction above. Let \(S_j(w)=\max\{w\cdot y: y\in F_j\}\) be the support function of \(F_j\).

The large-market OSP design problem can be written as the convex program
\[\min_{(x_j)_{j\in J}}\ \sum_{j\in J}\pi_j\cdot x_j\quad\text{s.t.}\quad x_j\in F_j\ \forall j,\ \sum_{j\in J}x_j=(n_1,\dots,n_I),\]

\[\sup_{\lambda\in\mathbb{R}^I}\ \sum_{i=1}^I\lambda_i n_i-\sum_{j\in J}S_j(\lambda-\pi_j).\]
Evaluating or optimizing over the mechanism for each agent \(j\) therefore reduces to characterizing the support function \(S_j\), i.e. solving an optimization problem whose decision variables are menus of polarized rays and selection rules.

Determine whether the optimization problem of characterizing \(S_j(w)=\max\{w\cdot y: y\in F_j\}\) (equivalently, optimizing over polarized-ray menus and selection rules for a given agent \(j\) in the large-market relaxation) can be solved efficiently as a function of \((w,\eta_j,\text{the distribution of }c_j)\). In particular, establish the computational complexity of computing \(S_j(w)\) (e.g. polynomial-time solvable vs. NP-hard) and/or design an algorithm with provable complexity guarantees for approximating \(S_j(w)\) to a specified accuracy.
open_problem_quote: Despite these simplifications, the extent to which \(S_j\) can be efficiently characterized remains an open question, which we leave for future work.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is primarily an algorithmic/complexity classification question (e.g., proving NP-hardness or designing a polynomial-time algorithm), not a conjecture about existence of a finite counterexample instance. Automated searches may help guess hardness via reductions but do not directly yield a definitive resolution.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate algorithms for approximating \(S_j(w)\) can be represented as code and evaluated on simulated instances with a computable objective value as a fitness signal. However, the ultimate goal concerns worst-case efficiency/complexity guarantees, which requires proof beyond empirical performance, so automated evolution would be supportive rather than decisive.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
